title: Castle in the Air - Chapter Six: Which shows how Abdullah went from the frying pan into the fire (Fragment 1)
text:
Castle in the Air

Chapter Six

W HICH SHOWS HOW A BDULLAH WENT FROM THE FRYING PAN INTO THE FIRE

They put Abdullah in a deep and smelly dungeon where the only light came through a tiny grating high up in the ceiling—and that light was not daylight. It probably came from a distant window at the end of a passage on the floor above, where the grating was part of the floor.

Knowing that this was what he had to look forward to, Abdullah tried, as the soldiers dragged him away, to fill his eyes and mind with images of light. In the pause while the soldiers were unlocking the outside door to the dungeons, he looked up and around. They were in a dark little courtyard with blank walls of stone standing like cliffs all about it. But if he tipped his head tight back, Abdullah could just see a slender spire in the mid-distance, outlined against the rising gold of morning. It amazed him to see that it was only an hour after dawn. Above the spire the sky was deep blue with just one cloud standing peacefully in it. Morning was still flushing the cloud red and gold, giving it the look of a high-piled castle with golden windows. Golden light caught the wings of a white bird circling the spire. Abdullah was sure this was the last beauty he would ever see in his life. He stared backward at it as the soldiers lugged him inside.

He tried to treasure this image when he was locked in the cold gray dungeon, but it was impossible. The dungeon was another world. For a long time he was too miserable even to notice how cramped he was in his chains. When he did notice, he shifted and clanked about on the cold floor, but it did not help very much.

“I have to look forward to a lifetime of this,” he told himself. “Unless someone rescues Flower-in-the-Night, of course.” That did not seem likely, since the Sultan refused to believe in the djinn.

After this he tried to stave off despair with his daydream. But somehow, thinking of himself as a prince who had been kidnapped helped not at all. He knew it was untrue, and he kept thinking guiltily that Flower-in-the-Night had believed him when he told her. She must have decided to marry him because she thought he was a prince—being a princess herself, as he now knew. He simply could not imagine himself ever daring to tell her the truth. For a while it seemed to him that he deserved the worst fate the Sultan could invent for him.

Then he began thinking of Flower-in-the-Night herself. Wherever she was, she was certainly at least as scared and miserable as he was himself. Abdullah yearned to comfort her. He wanted to rescue her so much that he spent some time wrenching uselessly at his chains.

“For certainly nobody else is likely to try,” he muttered. “I must get out of here!”

Then, although he was sure it was another notion as silly as his daydream, he tried to summon the magic carpet. He visualized it lying on the floor of his booth, and he called to it, out loud, over and over again. He said all the magic-sounding words he could think of, hoping one of them would be the command word.

Nothing happened. And how silly to think that it would! Abdullah thought. Even if the carpet could hear him from the dungeon, supposing he got the command word right at last, how could even a magic carpet wriggle its way in here through that tiny grating? And suppose it did wriggle in, how would that help Abdullah to get out?

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Six: Which shows how Abdullah went from the frying pan into the fire

vocabulary.definition: To surprise someone greatly; fill with astonishment.
vocabulary.example: It amazed him to see that it was only an hour after dawn.

vocabulary.definition: To move all the way around something in a circle.
vocabulary.example: Golden light caught the wings of a white bird circling the spire.

vocabulary.definition: To make a loud, sharp metallic sound by striking something.
vocabulary.example: He shifted and clanked about on the cold floor.

vocabulary.definition: To soothe or ease the distress or sadness of someone.
vocabulary.example: Abdullah yearned to comfort her.

vocabulary.definition: A specific word used to control or command a magical or technical device.
vocabulary.example: He said all the magic-sounding words hoping one would be the command word.

vocabulary.definition: An open space surrounded by buildings or walls, especially in a castle or palace.
vocabulary.example: They were in a dark little courtyard with blank walls of stone standing like cliffs all about it.

vocabulary.definition: Uncomfortably small or restricted in space.
vocabulary.example: He was too miserable even to notice how cramped he was in his chains.

vocabulary.definition: To have earned or be worthy of something.
vocabulary.example: For a while it seemed to him that he deserved the worst fate the Sultan could invent for him.

vocabulary.definition: The complete loss or absence of hope.
vocabulary.example: He tried to stave off despair with his daydream.

vocabulary.definition: A dark, often underground room or cell used as a prison.
vocabulary.example: They put Abdullah in a deep and smelly dungeon where the only light came through a tiny grating.

vocabulary.definition: The development of events beyond a person's control; destiny.
vocabulary.example: He deserved the worst fate the Sultan could invent for him.

vocabulary.definition: To become reddened or to colour with a flush, often due to light.
vocabulary.example: Morning was still flushing the cloud red and gold.

vocabulary.definition: A framework of metal bars placed over an opening such as a window or drain.
vocabulary.example: The only light came through a tiny grating high up in the ceiling.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows or feels guilt.
vocabulary.example: He kept thinking guiltily that Flower-in-the-Night had believed him.

vocabulary.definition: To abduct and hold captive unlawfully.
vocabulary.example: Thinking of himself as a prince who had been kidnapped helped not at all.

vocabulary.definition: To carry or drag something heavy or cumbersome.
vocabulary.example: He stared backward at it as the soldiers lugged him inside.

vocabulary.definition: To speak quietly or indistinctly, often complaining or talking to oneself.
vocabulary.example: For certainly nobody else is likely to try,” he muttered.

vocabulary.definition: An idea, belief, or opinion about something.
vocabulary.example: He was sure it was another notion as silly as his daydream.

vocabulary.definition: To show or define the outer edge or shape of something clearly.
vocabulary.example: A slender spire in the mid-distance, outlined against the rising gold of morning.

vocabulary.definition: To save someone from a dangerous or distressing situation.
vocabulary.example: Unless someone rescues Flower-in-the-Night, of course.

vocabulary.definition: A tapering, pointed structure on the top of a building, typically a church or castle.
vocabulary.example: He could just see a slender spire in the mid-distance, outlined against the rising gold of morning.

vocabulary.definition: To call upon to do something or to appear.
vocabulary.example: He tried to summon the magic carpet.

vocabulary.definition: To form a mental image of something.
vocabulary.example: He visualized it lying on the floor of his booth.

vocabulary.definition: To twist and turn with small rapid movements, especially to get through a small space.
vocabulary.example: How could even a magic carpet wriggle its way in here through that tiny grating?
